summary: A podcast interview with Kova, an Instagram short-form creator known for getting millions of views, where she reveals her complete AI-powered content creation workflow. She demonstrates how to use Manus AI to reverse-engineer any creator's visual style into a replication plan, how to use Freepik's Nana Banana Pro model to transform plain backgrounds into visually stunning sets, and how to generate AI video transitions for scroll-stopping hooks. She also shares her system of using Obsidian + Claude Code to turn scripts into storyboards using templates and style guides. Target audience: content creators and solo entrepreneurs who want professional-looking short-form video without Hollywood budgets. Biggest takeaways: (1) AI image gen can replace expensive studio sets -- Kova built her entire visual brand from a dorm room, (2) Use AI agents like Manus to analyze and deconstruct creators you admire into actionable style guides, (3) Systems and templates (Obsidian + AI coding tools) are how solo creators scale content production.

insights.title: The AI Video Style Reverse-Engineering Workflow
insights.explanation: Feed a creator's video into Manus AI with a structured prompt: (1) extract style and aesthetic keywords, (2) transcribe and segment the script into story sections, (3) generate a replication plan. This produces a complete style guide covering typography, lighting, B-roll types, music tone, and narrative structure that you can adapt for your own content.

insights.title: The Two-Workflow System for 90% of Edits
insights.explanation: Kova's entire visual production relies on just two repeatable workflows: (1) AI background enhancement -- take a still frame from footage, run it through Freepik's image gen to add objects and set dressing, mask the result, and overlay it in Premiere Pro; (2) AI transitions -- extract start and end frames, generate a 3-4 second video clip between them using models like Kling or Kaiber, then stitch it into the edit. These two techniques account for 90% of her most impressive shots.

insights.title: Studio-Quality Sets No Longer Require a Studio
insights.explanation: Kova built her entire visual brand -- fairy lights, vinyl players, bookshelves, lush plant arrangements -- from a bare dorm room using AI image generation. Hollywood-level production design is no longer gatekept by budget or physical space; any creator with a dark room and AI tools can achieve it.

insights.title: Simpler Prompts Produce Better Image Gen Results
insights.explanation: When prompting image generation models, shorter and more general prompts (e.g., 'more flowers' or 'orange tulips in a vase') tend to produce better output because the model defaults to what it is actually good at. Overly specific prompts force the model into territory where it is more likely to fail.

insights.title: Avoid Negative Language in Video Gen Prompts
insights.explanation: Video generation models do not truly comprehend negation. Prompts like 'the camera doesn't move' will often cause the camera to move, because the model latches onto the keyword 'move' regardless of the 'doesn't.' Use positive phrasing instead -- say 'camera is static' or 'camera stationary.'

insights.title: The Five-Act Short-Form Video Structure
insights.explanation: Viral short-form content follows a five-act build-log structure: (1) Hook -- show the finished product first and state the concept in under 10 seconds, (2) Conflict/Stakes -- introduce a personal challenge for relatability, (3) The Build -- show 2-3 key process steps, (4) Problem and Resolution -- a comedic or dramatic arc (too slow, too fast, just right), (5) Tease/CTA -- creative call to action. This mirrors the hero's journey compressed into 60-75 seconds.

insights.title: Every Video Needs a One-Sentence Concept That Is Both Technical and Emotional
insights.explanation: The strongest short-form videos have a concept that can be explained in under 10 seconds and has an immediate visual payoff. The concept must combine a technical mechanic with an emotionally resonant outcome -- e.g., 'a flash drive whose character gets chunkier the more files you add.' Define this before filming anything.

insights.title: Differentiation Is the New Scale
insights.explanation: Creative culture has become so fragmented that scale alone no longer wins. Creators of the future must prioritize differentiation over reach. Kova's differentiator is artistry amplified by AI -- a combination that is hard to replicate. Every creator needs to identify their own differentiating factor and use AI tools to amplify it.

insights.title: The Obsidian + Claude Code Content System
insights.explanation: Keep templates (storyboard, editor handoff, style guide) in an Obsidian vault. When a script is ready, open the vault in Claude Code or Cursor and prompt it to transform the script into the storyboard template. The AI can also restructure folders, convert file types, and do a first-pass edit following your documented style guide. This turns static notes into an executable content production system.

insights.title: Systems Are the Prerequisite to Scaling Content
insights.explanation: To scale content production as a solo creator, you must first build documented systems -- style guides, templates, folder structures, naming conventions. AI tools like Claude Code can then execute on those systems, but only if the systems are well-documented first. Without systems, AI assistance remains ad hoc and unrepeatable.
